title: Speculaas
subtitle: Dutch spiced shortbread biscuits with cinnamon, ginger, cloves, and cardamom
excerpt: Thin, crisp Dutch spiced biscuits made from a butter shortbread base with a complex blend of warm spices and a little white pepper. The dough is rested overnight so the spice flavours develop before baking. In the Netherlands they are pressed into carved wooden moulds; here the dough is rolled and cut into shapes. Flaked almonds are pressed into the surface before baking. The biscuits should be very thin and completely crisp.
sourceNotes: Speculaas (also spelled speculoos or speculaas) are Dutch and Belgian spiced biscuits associated with Sinterklaas (St. Nicholas Day, 5 December). They are documented in Dutch cookery literature from at least the seventeenth century. The spice blend of cinnamon, ginger, cloves, cardamom, and white pepper is consistent across Dutch baking sources from the nineteenth century. Mrs Elisabeth Eybers-van der Horst, Nederlandsch Kookboek (1855), gives a recipe for speculaas consistent with the modern version. The biscuits became internationally known through the Belgian brand Biscoff (speculoos paste), though the home-baking version predates it by centuries.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked speculaas for up to 2 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the dough a day or two ahead and refrigerate. The longer the rest, the more developed the spice flavour. Baked biscuits keep in an airtight tin for 2 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 155°C. Bake for 12 to 16 minutes until evenly deep golden. At 3mm thickness they will be fully crisp once cool; thicker biscuits may need 2 more minutes.

recipeTools.notes: two baking trays, lined with baking paper

glossaryTerms.definition: The aromatic mixture of ground cinnamon, ginger, cloves, cardamom, and white pepper (and sometimes nutmeg and mace) that defines speculaas. The blend is more complex than British mixed spice and has a distinctly warm, slightly spicy and bitter edge from the white pepper and cloves. The flavours develop and deepen during a dough rest of 8 to 24 hours as the spices penetrate the flour and fat. Freshly made dough tastes slightly raw and harsh; rested dough is rounded and aromatic. The proportions given here are a starting point; adjust them to taste.

content.text: The character of speculaas comes from its
and an overnight dough rest. Roll the chilled dough as thin as you dare: 3mm gives a properly crisp result. Any thicker and the biscuits may be soft rather than snappy.

The day before: rub the cold butter into the flour until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Stir in the sugar, bicarbonate of soda, all the spices, and salt. Beat the egg and stir it in to bring the dough together. It will be crumbly at first; press and knead briefly until it comes together into a smooth dough. Flatten into a disc, wrap, and refrigerate overnight.

Heat the oven to 175°C conventional (155°C fan). Line two baking trays. Roll the chilled dough on a lightly floured surface to 3mm thick. Cut into rectangles or shapes. Arrange on the trays and press a few flaked almonds into the top of each. Bake for 12 to 16 minutes until evenly deep golden. Cool on a rack; they will crisp up fully once cool. Store in an airtight tin.
